description: Generic programming and the standard library: function and class templates, deduction and specialisation, then the STL — containers (vector, map, set), iterators, the <algorithm> header and lambdas. Every card is answerable from the primer.
primer:
## What this set covers
How C++ writes type-generic code (templates) and the standard library built on it (the STL). Every card traces back to this page.

## Templates
- A **function template** is a pattern the compiler **instantiates** into a concrete function per type: `template <typename T> T max(T a, T b) { return a > b ? a : b; }`. `typename` and `class` are interchangeable in the parameter list.
- A **class template** parameterises a class on types: `template <typename T> class Box { T value; };`; `Box<int>` and `Box<std::string>` are separate instantiations. STL containers are class templates.
- **Argument deduction**: the compiler infers parameters from the call (`max(3, 4)` → `T = int`), so you rarely write `<T>` explicitly. Since C++17, class templates deduce too (CTAD). A single `T` must be deduced consistently: `max(3, 4.0)` **fails** (int vs double) — force `max<double>(...)`, cast, or use two type parameters.
- **Specialisation** gives a distinct implementation for a specific argument: full `template <> class Box<bool> {...}`, or **partial** `template <typename T> class Box<T*> {...}` (a pattern; class templates only — function templates overload instead).
- A **non-type template parameter** is a compile-time value, e.g. the `N` in `std::array<int, N>`.
- Each distinct argument set is a separate **instantiation** (its own machine code → possible code bloat). Template **definitions live in headers** (the compiler needs the full definition at instantiation). Errors typically surface **at instantiation**, not at the definition.
- A **generic lambda** `[](auto x){...}` is sugar for a templated call operator — templates and `auto` share the same deduction machinery.
- **Trade-off vs virtuals**: templates resolve at compile time (fast, inlined, but bloated + cryptic errors); virtual functions resolve at runtime (one indirection, one shared implementation, smaller binary).

## STL containers
- **`std::vector`**: growable, **contiguous** array that knows its size; `push_back` is amortised $O(1)$; the **default** sequence container. Growing past capacity **reallocates and moves** elements, **invalidating** existing iterators/pointers/references — reserve up front or re-fetch.
- **`std::map`**: ordered (balanced tree), keys sorted, $O(\log n)$ ops. **`std::unordered_map`**: hash table, average $O(1)$, no ordering. **`std::set`** / **`unordered_set`**: collections of unique keys (sorted-tree vs hashed). Safe lookup with `m.find(key)` (returns `end()` if absent) — beware `m[key]` **inserts** a default value when the key is missing.
- **`std::list`** (doubly linked) has **no random access** (no `operator[]`); its strength is $O(1)$ insert/remove at a known position.

## Iterators & algorithms
- An **iterator** is a pointer-like object traversing a container: `begin()` to the first element, `end()` to **one past the last**. Ranges are **half-open** `[begin, end)`; `++it` advances, `*it` dereferences.
- **`<algorithm>`** provides generic range algorithms: `std::sort`, `std::find`, `std::count`/`count_if`, `std::for_each`, `std::transform`, `std::copy`, `std::remove`; `std::accumulate` (in `<numeric>`) folds a range to one value (sum by default, custom op otherwise). Sort descending with a comparator: `std::sort(b, e, [](int a, int b){ return a > b; });`.
- A **lambda** `[capture](params){ body }` is an inline anonymous function — the natural callable for algorithms. The **capture list** says which outer variables it uses and how: `[x]` by copy, `[&x]` by reference, `[=]`/`[&]` everything by copy/reference. **Capturing by reference into a lambda that outlives the variable dangles** — capture by value instead.
